author: @Tekisasubakani
text:
Years ago I worked as a bellman/valet at a hotel with a guy who was tall, dark and movie star handsome.  One slow day we were in the back office just shooting the breeze, and he starts going on about being tired of [predominantly] middle-aged female guests hitting on him.  He went on for a bit, and ended with a "you know what I mean?"  I sat there stunned for a moment, then flatly told him "No, no I don't know what you mean, I'd kill to have even one woman hit on me so blatently."  I suppose he'd never considered what it was like to be part of the "other half", so that stunned him enough to change the subject.  

People will complain about being attractive so often, yet if given the choice between that and being "ugly", how many people are going to choose the latter?  You can't have your cake and eat it too.

replies.text: I love Ryan Reynolds. I'll watch pretty much anything he's in. Same with Jason Statham. But I don't go into their movies expecting them to not be the characters they play regularly. Each braches out occasionally, but even as a kind father, you expect Jason Statham to fight in movies. And even if a character Ryan Reynolds plays is serious, you expect some jokes. I was a impressed by Amityville horror in Reynolds case, but the movie set me up to know that he wasn't going to be a Ryan Reynolds movie. But here's the thing from Van Wilder up to Deadpool, Ryan Reynolds delivers what everybody wants from Ryan Reynolds. In a Ryan Reynolds movie, I want a sarcastic, hot, often nerdy (ie Smart) guy making fun of dumb guys and hooking up with hot girls. I'm actually never expecting Oscar material out of Ryan Reynolds. Now if he ever delivers that, effing good on him. I'll see the movie and be amazed. But I go to his movies to see him play that character. Movies are escapism. They give us a chance for us to live a different experience for a few hours, as long as we can relate to the heroes journey in the film. If we don't relate to a character in the film, the film sucks. See all the miserable examples of main characters in movies who no one relates to from the last 5 years.

author: @chrismaddock5790
text: I remember seeing an old archive interview that Peter Cushing did once when he was at the height of his Hammer Horror movie fame (I think it was promoting frankenstein or something). The interviewer asked: "Are you worried about being type casted into what is essentially the same role for each film?" Cushing replied, after a bout of fake crying and hearty laughter: "As long as it gets me work, then honestly I don't feel it's anything too bad. I guess it's something I'd have to worry about at another time in my life, but by then I hope to be well off enough to retire anyway, or at the very least be comfortable enough to play smaller roles/movies". This was when his wife was still alive (she was by his side during the interview, I think) and in arguably a much better place in his life at that point, but my god I love seeing that man, whether it's in movies or interviews.

Originally, yes. The Black Widow trope was based around the "honey trap" concept employed in espionage. A woman would sleep with a man to get intel and/or to assassinate.

They get to break out into much more fulfilling roles that allow them to delve into their acting abilities more later in life when they aren't being typecast. This is amazing. And also mirrors regular people's jobs as well. Early careers suck ass, and you are often doing shit you don't want to really be doing, in hopes to get to where you actually want to be, working on projects you actually want to be working on. Middle age is when everything starts to come together (hopefully). A career takes time, hard work, and often trudging through the mud. Actresses at this stage may feel icky from viewing this as being exploited rather than the other way around. She used her beauty, knowing people want to see beauty, and managed to become one of the highest paid actresses in Hollywood. As someone still building their career, walking through the muck, I hope to get to my dream job one day. I just face different challenges and hurdles to overcome.

text: I mean, I get it. I went to theater school, and they told me I would always be archetyped based on how I look. I played fantastic and intense villains in theater, but the instructors took one look at me and said, "You're gonna be playing underdogs for your entire career." I was just like, you know what, I don't think I wanna participate in that. So I didn't. If you choose to stay in the industry, you've gotta know you're gonna be stereotyped, and it takes a ton of success and fame and favors and money to start to make that turn. Look to the long term, and you'll get those intense acting roles you want.

replies.author: @cozyJuice
replies.text: I find it kind of strange that you listened to those people and ended your acting career before it even began. You could have gotten roles, grown, learned, and maybe become something real. I can imagine the situation felt like a reality check, but it was their reality, not yours.
